title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the printing press. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Gutenberg Revolution: Information for All
content.text: Before the middle of the 15th century, books were a luxury reserved for the ultra-wealthy and the clergy. Each volume was painstakingly copied by hand by scribes, a process that could take months or even years for a single Bible. This labor-intensive method meant that knowledge was effectively locked away in monasteries and royal libraries, far from the reach of the common person. ||(41)|| [41]Johannes Gutenberg, a goldsmith from Mainz, Germany, changed everything[/41]. Drawing on his experience with precision metalworking, he developed a system of 'movable type.' Instead of carving an entire page into a single block of wood, he cast individual letters in a durable lead alloy that could be rearranged and reused thousands of times. ||(42)|| [42]This mechanical efficiency was combined with the use of a modified wine press and a new, oil-based ink that adhered better to metal than the watery inks used for calligraphy[/42]. In 1455, Gutenberg produced his masterpiece: the 42-line Bible. While only about 180 copies were printed, it proved that the machine could produce text of a quality that rivaled the finest hand-written manuscripts. ||(43)|| [43]The impact of this technology was immediate and explosive[/43]. By 1500, printing presses had spread to over 200 cities across Europe, churning out millions of books. This 'information explosion' fueled the Renaissance, as classical Greek and Roman texts were rediscovered and distributed to a hungry new class of educated laypeople. ||(44)|| [44]This shift in literacy had profound religious and political consequences[/44]. For the first time, individuals could read the scriptures for themselves rather than relying on the interpretation of a priest. This helped spark the Reformation, as thinkers like Martin Luther used the press to spread their revolutionary ideas at a speed the established Church could not control. ||(45)|| [45]As the centuries progressed, the press also became the birthplace of the modern newspaper[/45]. The ability to distribute news and political debate to a wide audience created the 'public sphere,' a space where government policies could be openly criticized and discussed. The printing press was no longer just a tool for scholars; it was a weapon of democracy. ||(46)|| [46]Today, we are living through a similar transformation with the rise of the internet[/46]. While the medium has changed from ink and paper to pixels and light, the fundamental result is the same: the radical democratization of information. Gutenberg’s legacy is found in every screen we touch, reminding us that when knowledge is shared, the world changes forever.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] the high cost of production [/41] created a significant barrier to education. A single book could cost as much as a small farm, ensuring that the vast majority of the population remained illiterate and disconnected from the intellectual currents of their time.
paragraphs.E: This [42] innovative use of metal [/42] was the key to the revolution. By creating standardized, interchangeable parts, Gutenberg applied the principles of mass production to the written word centuries before the Industrial Revolution, making the spread of ideas both fast and affordable.
paragraphs.C: Beyond [43] these early religious texts [/43], the press began to produce secular works, including scientific treatises and travelogues. The standardization of language and spelling that followed helped create a sense of national identity among people who previously spoke disparate local dialects.
paragraphs.D: The [44] democratization of knowledge [/44] also meant that scientific discoveries could be shared and verified across borders. Scientists like Copernicus and Galileo could publish their findings, ensuring that their work reached a global community of peers rather than being lost in a single library.
paragraphs.F: The [45] speed of this dissemination [/45] was unprecedented. A pamphlet printed in Germany could reach the streets of London or Paris in a matter of weeks, making it impossible for authorities to suppress dissent or maintain a monopoly on the 'truth.'
paragraphs.G: This [46] transition to a mass-media society [/46] was finalized during the 19th century with the invention of the steam-powered press. This allowed for the production of 'penny papers,' making the news accessible to even the poorest citizens and forever changing the nature of political campaigning.
paragraphs.B: Many [47] traditional scribes initially feared [/47] that the machine would destroy the beauty of the book. they argued that the soul of a text was found in the unique variations of a human hand, and that mass-produced volumes were a soulless imitation of true art.

questions.explanation: A explains the 'high cost of production' and the 'barrier to education' caused by the hand-copying process described in the first paragraph.
E refers to the 'innovative use of metal' (the lead alloy type) and the principle of 'standardized parts' mentioned by Gutenberg.
C refers to the 'early religious texts' (like the 42-line Bible) and explains how the press moved into 'secular works' and 'scientific treatises'.
D links the 'democratization of knowledge' to the sharing of 'scientific discoveries' among the 'educated laypeople' mentioned in the text.
F explains the 'speed of dissemination' that allowed ideas to spread faster than 'authorities could control', linking to the Reformation context.
G describes the 'transition to a mass-media society' and the 'penny papers', which connects the history of the press to the 'weapon of democracy' concept.
